sentences.text: Yes, well, I returned, as I said, to my barrack, not knowing whether the matter will-- was resolved or not, whether I'll be taken; or instead of me, somebody else.
I had hopes.
And as they called us out, we were taken into the trains.
And we were sure we are going to Brunnlitz, in Czechoslovakia.
We were traveling three days, I think.
And when we finally arrived to a destination, it was nighttime.
All you could see is arbeit macht frei-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --lots and lots of barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we realized that we are in Auschwitz.
Now that was really-- that was the last straw in my bonnet, if I can say so.
That's-- I felt that that's the last thing I can take.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sometimes mid-October or around mid-October-- I can't remember exactly the date.
But whether it was the 10th or 15th-- I don't want to lie, because I don't remember.
We were absolutely flabbergasted.
We were-- we were-- we don't-- we didn't talk.
We just looked at each other.
Yeah, it was terrible.
They put us in the blocks there, and ugh-- it was shocking.
When we came in?
Oh!
They took us, they-- ugh!
We had to go and have showers.
A shower was just drip, drip of cold water-- just every here a-- here and there a drip of water.
I wouldn't mind the cold water if it will be water, but a drip here and a drip there was absolutely unbearable.
Then, of course, they took us-- naked-- to have our hair cut.
And there were Hungarian women who worked with the Germans there.
And being naked, no matter under what circumstances, even in Auschwitz, you do feel embarrassed.
And I said, I have got clean hair, why do you cut my hair?
I don't have lice.
So she hit me, that-- if I stayed-- if everything there stayed in place, that was a miracle.
And the SS-- and she-- she really hurt me badly, and pushed me.
And the SS man came, and said, what's going on here?
I said, I don't have lice.
And my hair was all cut, shaven off-- only that little bit here.
He said to leave it.
So I suppose I'm lucky or something.
Luck has a lot to do with everything in life.
And--
Well, on the way out to Brunnlitz, I was trying to be [? all what I-- ?] each time it came close, they told me I was going to the end.
And then, I just simply just-- the minute I walked away and sucked it up.
I-- I don't think-- none of us actually have it.
It must have been either they were short at-- of something, maybe of the stuff that-- I don't know.
Because other ladies that were in Brunnlitz-- I-- just to play-- be on the safe side, I was-- kept sucking it.
But I just-- I just have to ask the others, how come that they haven't got it either?
What was your number?
My number's on the list.
I-- I don't remember it by heart.
But it's on the list that-- the-- the list comes from the German authorities-- the list of-- that's why the movie was called And then--
I got typhus in Auschwitz.
But of course, in Auschwitz, or any other concentration camp, you can't admit that you are sick if you want to live.

And even though I could hardly walk or keep my head up, and I had diarrhea, and we were wearing the clogs-- which one was always too long and the other was too small-- it was just running right through me.
But there was a-- what they called the block altester-- the-- the-- the one responsible for the block.
It was a Polish woman prostitute.
She was already too old to be a prostitute, so she was the madam-- hardly looked like one, but in Auschwitz, we all looked the same.
And as they say, that saints and prostitutes have got soft hearts, well, she helped me a lot with the-- I told her that I am sick.
And you know what she was doing?
She had a doona, and each time, she ripped out a little bit, because we didn't have toilet paper.
So a credit to her, whoever she was.
And then she kept telling me all the time, don't tell anybody that you are sick.
But I had [? a hard-- I-- ?] when we got to the train, I was already delirious.
But again, I was lucky.
They had a bench there for-- for them to sit down.
They put me on a bench.
They poured hot water into my mouth.
The girls said they couldn't believe it.
I was singing away the Polish anthem, [NON-ENGLISH].
I was cursing them.
And the girls put their hands on my mouth.
And they said, let her talk.
When really, they-- of course, they didn't understand Polish.
That's another story.
That's when Schindler has sent one of his mistresses to Berlin to a-- he has heard-- he asked where the-- where the women are.
And somehow-- and the word-- a word got to him that we are in Auschwitz.
So he sent the-- one of his mistresses-- I don't remember which one it was-- to Berlin.
And he-- he-- we-- that was the only case that the list was not only numbers, but by names, also.
And he said he has to help the women right now.
And she got the permission from Berlin, whoever it was-- Goebbels, Goring, or one of them.
And I believe that she came to Auschwitz to the-- to see Hess, or whoever was there at that moment.
And that's how she got [? that out. ?] But she got-- she got there a minute to 12:00.
Because we were already lined up to the crematorium with the others.
You won't believe me if I tell you, but it was just the beginning of the Schindler women, and I was in the first row with some others.
And Dr.-- the infamous Dr.
Mengele, just put his, whatever you call it, this leather thing that they will use to beat us with-- he put it against me.
And he said, for today, it's enough.
And that night, we left Auschwitz.
So obviously, you know, the letter must have-- or something must have reached him that we are to be saved.
Oh, we had to go to the role platz, and they counted us 100 times, over and over, and over again.
They-- they look-- when we arrived, each time.
On each occasion they looked.
If you had a little something on your body, you are gone.
But thanks to my great parents, I was well nourished, well looked after.
I was strong.
And maybe that's why my body kept well.
And I, of course, washed myself wherever I had the opportunity to get a bit of water-- if it was raining, where we were getting the food.
And I used that for the rain water to wash my hair, or to wash my body.
When we got to Brunnlitz-- well, I had typhus, and another girl had typhus.
We were-- of course, over there we had to be honest, because it was more confined.
And I said that I have typhus-- or whatever it is.
I didn't know the name of it.
But we had doctors.
They were prisoners' doctors.
Oh, nothing elaborate-- and very cramped.
It was a factory.
And Schindler made-- cut it in such a way that the factory and the living quarters were together.
He didn't want-- I remember-- [LAUGHS] It's-- you know, when you talk about it, and you see it in your-- in front of your eyes, things are coming to my-- to your mind.
We-- I-- I couldn't talk about all that, because there isn't it enough time.
Schindler-- well, Schindler gave us extra rations of bread, and soup, and a real welcome.
And-- but bread was no good to me.
Mrs.
Schindler was bringing-- we were put a little bit in such a distance from the others, the healthy ones-- healthy, well-- but without typhus.
And Mrs.
Schindler was coming and-- and hiding, and bringing us some soups, some-- I suppose they were disprins, aspirins.
I don't know what they were-- whatever she could put her hands on.
Unfortunately, the girl next to me has died.
Again, I survived.
Why?
I don't know.
I wasn't better than any of them that died.
[CRYING] Did you see--
I often-- sorry?
I often think that maybe the punishment-- the punishment was to stay alive, because you have to go on living with all that.
It's so hard.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING]
Yes?
From Schindler?
Oh, yes, yes.
He-- he was a very kind man.
He-- he-- one night-- I was on night shift, always, there, because I was getting-- we were getting double portions.
And my brother was there, and Peter was there.
So I wanted a double portion.
And I don't know.
For some reason, I felt strong enough.
They were giving up all the time.
They have given up from the minute we were taken away.
So, one night, I was working.
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH], where I worked.
And someone put a arm around me.
I was petrified.
I thought it was somebody from the SS.
I looked around.
I said-- not to get frightened-- how are-- I'll speak in English.
How are you, Mrs.
Selinger?
And I said, I am fine, thank you.
And he gave me a little-- nicely-- nice little packet, with a little ribbon.
And I said, what have I done to deserve this?
It's Purim.
Now, it wasn't a-- it was a packet of cigarettes-- that-- that was all.
But-- it was so touching.
I didn't smoke at that time, of course.
The men had.
But it-- only the fact-- you know?
And he knew about the holidays, because he was terribly befriended before the war with that Itzhak Stern and-- and he knew the Jews.
And-- and even in Czechoslovakia, when he was young, he had friends at school who were Jewish, so--
--and his wife was strictly Catholic-- very observant.
There were no conditions for-- you mean work-wise?
He couldn't give a damn if-- not all of us, but if somebody was very observant, then he wouldn't mind.
He was-- but you see, not everybody knew who he was.
Like my sister-in-law's father was a religious Jew, but in the camps, he became really fanatic about it.
So we talked with the Itzhak Stern.
We called him [NON-ENGLISH] Stern.
And he said, look.
He's fanatic.
And he said, I'll-- I'll talk to Schindler.
But, really, he-- he wasn't a person that you-- sure they counted us, and it's just a procedure.
He couldn't let it run loose.
I mean, he did have inspections from time to time, you know.
But all in all, things could have been arranged with him.
He didn't mind.
One day-- it was November, December-- his wife came.
Well, I wasn't there-- but his-- pardon me-- his wife came and said that there is a train in the middle of the railway-- a rail is-- and people are wondering what it is.
And Schindler went with her.
And then they came back.
Because they-- there was no engine, no nothing, just the train-- the-- the wagons.
They could hear, voices.
So Schindler came back to the camp and took a few strong men with him, and who-- the medical people with him-- whoever was a doctor.
We had a few.
But the main one was Dr.
Biberstein.
And they tried to open.
It was frozen.
They couldn't open the train, because they were frozen.
You know how you have to open?
And so they had to really work on it.
And then when they opened, some fell out-- like corpses-- and some were just frozen.
And they sorted out who is alive, who is not.
The ones that were dead, Schindler arranged for them to be buried.
And the ones that had a sign of life in them, he took them to the camp and got the medical people to work on him-- on them.
Well, yes.
I mean, look.
There was 600 men with us.
I didn't know each-- each one of them.
And when they came out and joined the working force, I wouldn't know one from the other.
But we knew that they are there.
But of course, they were in-- we didn't have a hospital.
They were just living with the-- the men.
They would probably see the new arrivals.
I-- I hadn't seen them.
But there were 400 or-- 400, I think, of them.
[DRINKING] And it happened because they were losing the war, the Germans.
And-- pardon me.
And they tried to-- not to leave a trace after they cleaned out.
So they packed them into trains into the next camp.
Now, by the time they arrived there, the next camp didn't exist anymore either.
So they were just dumped there, because there was nowhere else to go anymore.
So it was their misfortune on one side, and fortune on the other hand, that Schindler-- Mrs.
Schindler had spotted them.
Well, then on the night-- on the night-- after midnight on the 8th of July-- from the 8th to the 9th of May-- sorry.
There was talk, like in the morning, I would say.
Not that I knew.
I-- I happened to see the-- first of all, there was a clock.
And you couldn't miss Schindler's watch-- we always admired his five, or six, or eight carat diamond ring.
Anyhow, there was no electricity that night.
The electricity was-- supply was coming from [NON-ENGLISH] not far away, and [NON-ENGLISH] was already finished.
So we didn't have electricity.
We knew-- you know, you can sense the atmosphere.
And you couldn't see an SS woman and not an SS man.
What's going on?
We didn't go to work.
We-- so he called us and told us that he is the happiest man to announce and tell us that we are the-- his Jews, who will be remembered in history as Schindler's Jews.
Because at this-- at this hour, the Germans have surrendered.
They have lost the war.
And then he just turned and said, I could have saved more.
I can see now.
Why didn't I?
And then he-- he-- well, we all cried.
You would think that we would jump of-- out of joy.
But that wasn't to be, because we didn't-- it was just like-- like a fairytale.
I hoped, always, that it will happen.
But when it does happen, you ask yourself, am I dreaming?
Is it true?
But then he asked someone to take him, to drive him to Pilsner, because he has to go to his headquarters.
And he-- nobody answered.
And he wanted also a letter confirming that he saved our life.
And nobody-- there was no reply.
Suddenly, everybody-- no, he said that he will pay with his ring.
Suddenly, everybody wanted to take him, but not for the ring.
They didn't want the ring.
Because he was taking it off.
But they didn't want it.
It was just the first reaction.
I suppose, just like I have asked myself whether is-- it's true or it's a dream, I suppose everybody did.
You know, you are a nobody, and suddenly you are free.
He only ask that we don't go outside, because there will still be Germans hidden somewhere, and to wait till the liberation army comes.
We hoped it would be the Americans.
Well, it wasn't.
The-- there were-- yeah, yeah-- the Russians, who treated us worse than the German women, they told us to peel potatoes.
And the German women were sitting doing nothing in their beautiful homes.
But the-- that was another story, again.
While Schindler is gone, everybody was crying when he waved good-bye.
And I was-- I was in Poland when he was in Israel, when the Jews brought him to Israel.
And in his will, you know that he wanted to be buried in Israel with his Jews.
He would-- I think he would be the happiest man to live in Israel.
The Russians.
And it was-- you know how the army, when it's-- occupying or marching into a-- in the-- into a place they have fought for-- since-- I don't know what you call it in English.
I knew the word, but I can't remember.
They sent first a-- nothing.
A man, a Russian officer came riding into the yard in-- on a horse.
And we were all excited, of course, but at the same time, afraid, too.
Because the Russians never appealed to any of us, although there were some that were running with red flags.
Even though Schindler said not to leave the camp, somebody was marching through the road-- like from here to the house there.
And they thought they were the Red Army.
And they ran out waving red flags, and they got shot.
Because there were still Germans running around.
Now, well-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
--there was-- when they marched in, he said to us, who are you?
And we said to them, we are Jewish people, and that's a camp.
And he said, listen.
I'm Jewish, too.
I shouldn't say it, but if you-- they-- don't register, because they may take you to Russia.
And in Russia, they don't like Jews, either.
And he just disappeared.
So we were sitting, and waiting, and contemplating.
Should we or shouldn't we?
Should we or shouldn't we?
So we were there, I don't know, about four weeks.
And-- finally, we had to register.
But no, they took us back to Cracow.
We'll break for the end of the tape there.
